steps.explanation: Compute $x^2 - y^2$ using substitution. Given $x^2 = a + b$ and $y^2 = a + c$: $x^2 - y^2 = (a + b) - (a + c) = b - c$. The $a$ terms cancel.
Expand using the perfect square formula $(p - q)^2 = p^2 - 2pq + q^2$: $(b - c)^2 = b^2 - 2bc + c^2$.

Evaluate each choice.
  • Choice A — $a^2 -2a c + c^2$: this would be $(a - c)^2$, but $a$ cancels out when computing $x^2 - y^2$. No $a$ should appear. Eliminate A.
  • Choice B — $b^2 -2b c + c^2$: this is exactly $(b - c)^2$, matching our derivation. This is correct.
  • Choice C — $4a^2 -4a b c + c^2$: contains $a$ terms and mixed coefficients inconsistent with $(b-c)^2$. Eliminate C.
  • Choice D — $4a^2 -2a b c + b^2c^2$: contains $a$ and a product $b^2c^2$ which cannot come from $(b-c)^2$. Eliminate D.
That confirms B is correct.
